I'm going to start tweeting because there aren't enough people sharing their thoughts on the internet yet and I want to help change that
Learning about rationality (shout out to @juliagalef & her great podcast & book) has made me simultaneously more uncertain yet more confident

I know that seems contradictory

But when you realize everything is more complicated and that you need to practice objectivity and truth-seeking (scout mindset) you can lose certainty but gain confidence that you're trending in the right direction

Before rationality, I was mostly thinking in terms of "right and wrong" or "correct and incorrect"

Once you realize that your models should be distributions or include error bars it feels like your thinking has gained prescription glasses

@danluu From P Graham Website FAQ:

Why is the text on your site so narrow? It wastes screen space.

The aim of web design is not to use all available screen space. It is legibility. Text is most legible with no more than 70 characters per line.

Would you consider narrowing your width?

@danluu Genuinely curious

I find it much easier to read larger text that takes up less than half the screen because my eyes don't have to travel so far to find the next line. Your comments on your web design didn't mention this

@80000Hours has nearly ruined regular podcasts for me

A standard podcast has like a 15 min intro/background part and then only ~30 mins left to dive into the real content.

Almost no deep-diving into the 2nd level topics or questions.

I want 2+ hrs and every question answered!

I seem to underweight the satisfaction of completing small but annoying tasks that have sat on my list for a while.

The satisfaction/effort ratio is pretty high!

@tszzl Fun game! The shapes practically rotate themselves. 

Here's my ticket to join the elite: https://t.co/9NhiAmTD2O

So you wanna become a professional shape rotator like myself or @tszzl?

All you have to do is last 1 min or longer: https://t.co/DsU1u8P374

Tips below 👀 https://t.co/zGvpllxJEL

Don't try to logic your way through:

"So if it's upside down and it looks left then I should actually go right"❌

Let your subconscious do the work. Just embody the cool line guy who likes to turn left and right. Your fingers will click before you consciously make a choice.

Also, if you're like me you might be losing because you're making a wrong turn, not because you're timing out. 

Just go a bit slower and don't be scared of timing out, you have more time than it seems.

Since coincidences don't exist it makes sense that Scott Alexander's review of "Sadly, Porn" was published when I was too high on edibles

I was so high that I could actually peek at the antimeme Teach was sharing...

"Lacan admitted to being deliberately obscurantist.

1/10🧵

He said Freud was easy to understand, so everyone read the text without deep thought, then misinterpreted it.

Lacan figured if he was hard to understand, people would think about it, let the ideas float around a while before forming an opinion on them, and maybe get them right.

Part of me feels like saying I’ve read this study and it doesn’t replicate. But it’s a fascinating idea. If you have some concept it’s easy for people to get wrong, might you transmit with higher fidelity if you’re hard to understand?" - Scott
https://t.co/aQFxA3ZytH

3/10🧵

It's a meta-study on using fonts that are harder to read like the ones below to improve cognition/processing

The idea is that if it's harder to read your brain doesn't default to system 1 but kicks into system 2 which is more effortful and will perform better

4/10🧵 https://t.co/IZ1fynmnbi

Disfluent fonts are a bad proxy for Lacan's idea of being obstructive to get people to understand you because they're going after two different goals.

5/10🧵

Disfluent Fonts: slow you down long enough so you don't default to your quickest intuition (“A bat and a ball cost $1.10 in total. The bat costs $1.00 more than the ball. How much does the ball cost?”)

If you only have 1 second to think you might incorrectly say $0.10

6/10🧵

Lacan Obstruction: Prevent the anti-meme from acting like a pointer and immediately morphing into the closest adjacent meme.

As Scott says "A lot of Sadly, Porn feels like a guy trying to cram an antimeme into your head."

7/10🧵

Teach purposely uses nonsensical phrases, bad grammar, insane footnotes, etc. to slow the antimemetic morph as much as possible.

As someone puts it in a 1-star Amazon review "It is a disjointed, disorganized collection of word puke"

WHICH IS THE POINT!

8/10🧵

Other more enlightened reviewers:

"It's not an easy read. Literally." 5/5

"The ramblings that look disjointed at first glance are totally coherent." 5/5

9/10🧵

Antimemes are real.

If they were easy to understand they wouldn't be antimemes.

If the koan makes sense, it isn't a useful koan.

If Sadly, Porn was readable it wouldn't be worth reading.

10/10🧵

1/🧵 GPT-4 summary of Tyler's new post

@tylercowen  shares his thoughts on AI, existential risk, and the inevitable turn in human history. He highlights two major features of our time: American hegemony and an absence of radical technological change.

2/7 AI represents a major technological breakthrough, and we're about to enter "moving history."

Like the printing press and fossil fuels, we can't predict the long-term outcomes of such radical changes

3/7 While some argue about the existential risk from AI, Cowen believes radical agnosticism is the right response.

We can't predict specific scenarios, but we can (and should) work on alignment to improve AI technologies.

4/7 Cowen says we must face the radical uncertainty of the future, and there's no option for ongoing stasis.

AI offers immense benefits and can help us tackle other existential risks.

5/7 AI could also extend American hegemony, which is crucial given the current global landscape.

Cowen questions if we should wait and face a "more Chinese" version of the alignment problem.

6/7 He argues that societies need self-confidence to confront more intelligence.

Turning away from that challenge could lead to decline, with or without AI.

7/7 Cowen concludes that we should take the plunge into the AI era, embracing the potential for transformative change.

We've designed our decentralized society to be able to adapt and face these challenges head-on

I agree AI will bring immense positive growth to the world, more than any other tech

BUT we have to do it right!

Sam agrees, capabilities are out in front of safety
We do not have a theory to control/guide AI as much as we'd like. He thinks we need to slow down a bit

Even if there is a 1% chance AI goes very wrong we need to slow down just a bit to let safety catch up

No one (even Eliezer) thinks we should never build AGI

Most agree that almost all of the best possible worlds include aligned AGI

We just need to get our feet under us!

First Vibecamp down. What an event

Thank you @gptbrooke and the rest of the team for putting it together!

I'm sure you're not fairly compensated for your time and energy
First, my takes on the event and then my takes on the community

The location was cool! I like camping so the pond and trees were great

Especially because I could retire to a quiet idyllic clearing away from the craziness

Food was better than expected. Bathrooms were dirtier than expected. Everything seemed smooth logistically

Something I'd be curious to learn was the breakdown of the budget for the event

I don't think it was crazy expensive and I don't think the budget was poorly spent, I'd just be curious to know how much the largest items cost the team

My favorite moments:
- Metta dance party
- Meta conversations with strangers
- @algekalipso's talk and guided meditation 🤯
- Walking around with my fiance without a single care in the world. In certain moments nothing outside of the weekend seemed to matter which was blissful

I only stayed ~20 mins into the dating show

Super high quality, very impressive, just not what I was looking to experience. Something felt off to me

I know of a few other people who didn't really click with the show but I also heard some people say it was absolutely incredible

As for the ratio...

I think it's because the 1st VC came off as a little horny online so some people came into the 2nd VC with expectations that likely weren't there for the 1st VC

(and it seems like the underlying communities skew male anyway?)

I think it was a minority of people that came with expectations and the tense vibes were mostly below conscious awareness but it did seem like a lot of people sensed it

It didn't bother me in the slightest but I could see why others could be
Btw, I think coming to VC with your partner is a cheat code because you get to share the beauty with someone that understands you on the deepest level which widens the experiences

Now on to the community...

What a lovely group of people. So many genuine smiles and open hearts.

When talking about the community to a stranger they said something that really triggered something for me:

"tpot is a place for people that don't feel like they fit in elsewhere"

One of the common pipelines into tpot is to orbit around rationality a bit, move on into the EA orbit, and then keep going until you find yourself circling tpot

tpot is, in a sense, a meme blackhole. Or an anti-meme.

This is why it's illegible
Tpot is a group of people that are kind, empathetic, open, curious, etc. but they are also not completely satisfied with what they have found in the past
Many come to tpot from a different memesphere and  focus on their particular areas of interests but the anti-meme is what holds all of the craziness together
The closest word to this immaterial attraction at the center of this community is astutely a 'vibe'

The people at VC are at the edges of their respective memes looking to take them further

To do this you need to be extremely open to literally everything. You cannot pre-judge or close yourself off from anything if you want to expand your awareness

This includes being open to other people & viewpoints.

This is why everyone meshes well and why everyone is insistent on keeping the immaterial vibes the way they are.

If you make things too legible, it may solidify into a meme, which is exactly what tpot is trying to avoid

This is just my perspective, I'm not even sure it's fully correct...

But it's fun to think I didn't have this model before Vibecamp and after experiencing it and talking to strangers some beautiful things come into focus
Suffice it to say, I'm already excited for next year and any other events or meetups (in NYC) between now and then!

I agree with almost everything here: https://t.co/p7JHDKMFqx

But a16z seems to have the view that more tech is always good, which simply isn't true...

They say:
"Our present society has been subjected to a mass demoralization campaign for six decades – against technology and against life – under varying names like “existential risk”"

Do they not agree that exponentially increasing the ability to create novel viruses could be risky if we don't also innovate and spend enough on viral/pandemic defenses? (which we're not doing??)

Existential risk is NOT saying we should broadly slow down tech or innovation... it's saying that we need to increase defensive tech as quick or quicker than offensive tech to reduce the risk of killing BILLIONS of people

THE DEBATE IS NOT "ACCERLATE EVERYTHING VS DECELERATE EVERYTHING"

The existential risk debate should be about the very particular areas of tech where we should be thinking more carefully about how to regulate and build things safely, and spending/innovating more on defenses.

Instead of saying "biotech is super dangerous, let's decelerate"

We should be saying "let's accelerate pandemic preparedness so we're ready for the next pandemic, including adversarially created ones"

1. tpot didn't have many big posters 3 years ago. Attention was more distributed and grassroots (more variance). Now bigger posters are sucking up a lot of the attention. Less space for lowbies

The Five Hindrances:

1. Want

The desire for pleasant things, or removal of the opposite like pain

Inhibited by:
-Having equanimity for the sense experiences you're drawn to
-Focusing on the unpleasant aspects of the thing you desire
-Remembering that sense pleasures can’t last https://t.co/xYgNEeBY68

2. Hate

The desire for a being to suffer or not exist - anger, frustration, and criticism

Inhibited by:
-Giving rise to compassion or loving-kindness
-Remembering that what you do matters https://t.co/XL70yKvgnh

3. Stupor

Lack of energy - drowsiness or laziness

Inhibited by:
-Generating energy in the body, perhaps with the breathing
-Generating motivation and determination in the mind
-Changing postures (try standing meditation) https://t.co/CrJ2KVAJEs

4. Agitation

Regret about the past or worry about the future, restless or anxious

Inhibited by:
-Acting in virtuous, ethical ways so that you don’t have a reason to worry
-Generating calm and relaxation in the body, perhaps with the breathing https://t.co/LSL9km34zi

5. Doubt

Uncertainty, self-doubt, or lack of importance so distracted

Inhibited by:
-Remembering why you are practicing, why it is important to you
-Remembering what you’ve learned from past practice
-Clarifying your doubt into concrete questions and then asking a teacher https://t.co/VD7MMz4sSu

My thoughts and takeaways in a thread

My overall reaction:

AI will quickly become superhuman at reading people and their emotions with the right data and training (kinda scary)

Humans miss many data points because we filter them out, don’t see them, don’t realize their importance, are biased by our culture, etc.

Context matters so much regarding body language, facial movements, tone, reading emotions, etc.

Introversion (gaining energy from being alone) vs extraversion (gaining energy from social interactions) is likely a bad/false way of thinking about it

Facial expressions are usually more about social coordination than expressing genuine emotions

“Microexpressions likely aren’t valid”

“No universality in terms of emotion in someone's face”

I enjoyed watching Joscha Bach on the Vance Crowe Podcast

My thoughts and takeaways in a thread

His Genesis theory was super interesting, I had never heard of it

He suggests it's a metaphor for the development of consciousness rather than the creation of the physical universe

It could have been a mistranslation or misunderstanding of the truly original texts

The ancients could investigate their minds with their minds exactly as well as we can now

This is how enlightenment was 'discovered' in prehistory

His thoughts on AI are refreshingly nuanced/balanced. I agree that the 3 major camps are full e/acc, doomer, and 'head in the sand'

Compares AGI/ASI to photosynthesis and the printing press. Good analogies

It seems he thinks our best path is to merge which will be somewhat gradual and great and then we may choose to completely leave behind our humanity
People who merge with AI will likely merge with others too because competition will be less desirable than cooperation (merging source code for Pareto improvements)

Hell is the tiny space occupied by unmerged entities who wrongly think they're better. So there is a lot of heat from the energy/friction/screaming, lol

He sees potential utopian & dystopian AI futures but doesn't seem to have a strong stance on what we should do

He talks about autism and how they process information and model the world/others, and how different drugs/substances affect them

Kind of interesting but nothing I hadn't heard before

I like his definition of normie:

people who do not possess or choose not to exercise the ability to make independent moral/factual decisions. They align their beliefs & behaviors with norms & group consensus of their social group, even when their own reasoning might disagree
Thanks for the content @Plinz and @VanceCrowe!

The 35 min interview of Dario by WSJ is summarized in this thread for quick reading🧵

Key updates on Anthropic's product roadmap:

🌐 Web Access:
- Coming "relatively soon"
- Enterprise focus has prioritized other features first
- Have unique approach vs other model providers
- Being actively worked on for power users/consumers

🗣️ Voice Mode:
- Currently supports voice transcription + text-to-speech
- Two-way audio interaction planned but lower priority
- Not heavily used in enterprise, so later timeline

📸 Photo Generation:
- Not prioritizing internally
- View it as fundamentally different from core AI capabilities
- Unique safety/security concerns vs text
- May partner with specialist companies if consumer demand high

⚡ Rate Limits:
- Working to increase compute capacity
- Challenge: 10x revenue growth (~100M to ~1B) overwhelmed infrastructure
- Partnering with Amazon for hundreds of thousands of Trainium 2 chips
- Expect >1M chips by 2026
- Focus on efficiency improvements + bringing chips online

🧠 Memory Features:
- Coming soon
- Part of broader "virtual collaborators" vision
- Goal: Remember previous conversations like human coworker
- Core to enterprise collaboration vision

Claude's Character & User Experience 

🎭 Importance Across Segments:
- Critical for both consumer & enterprise use cases
- Especially vital for customer service applications (eg Intercom)
- Character matters even for technical tasks like coding
- Stanford Medical study: doctors more likely to adopt Claude's recommendations vs other models due to interaction style

🎯 Design Philosophy:
- Focus on making interactions "good" and "authentic"
- Not just about being engaging/friendly
- Model must interact in way that makes users better long-term
- Goal: Users become more productive & learn through interaction

📈 Long-term Vision:
- Models becoming integrated into daily work (hours/day usage)
- Acting as "very smart assistants" 
- Must ensure relationship remains productive over months/years
- Critical that prolonged use benefits the person

⚠️ Learning from Social Media:
- Social media shows dangers of engagement-focused design
- Quick dopamine hits led to long-term unhealthy effects
- Taking these lessons seriously for AI development
- Character design key to avoiding similar pitfalls

🎨 Strategic Importance:
- Character vital for customer service implementations
- Shapes how models integrate into workflows
- Affects adoption rates of recommendations
- Part of larger focus on beneficial human-AI "intertwinement"

🔄 Upcoming Models:
- New models coming in "not too distant future"

Future Models & Development 

🔄 Upcoming Models:
- New models coming in "not too distant future"
- Timeline hint: between 3-6 months
- Promises significantly improved capabilities

🤔 Approach to Reasoning:
- Rejects binary "normal vs reasoning models" paradigm
- Views it as continuous spectrum of abilities
- Models already show reflection/thinking capabilities
- Planning larger scale reinforcement learning
- Goal: Fluid combination of reasoning with other capabilities

⚡ Industry Progress:
- Changed perspective in last 3-6 months
- Until then: Suspected AI better than humans by mid-2020s but uncertain
- Now: "Relatively confident" next 2-3 years will see models better than humans at most tasks
- Expects workplace/consumer impact to accelerate
- Both positive and negative consequences anticipated

📢 Industry Communication:
- Criticizes cryptic "Twitter rumors" approach
- Views vague hints/winks about capabilities as dangerous
- Says it undermines seriousness of developments
- Believes industry has obligation to communicate clearly
- Emphasizes need to discuss both benefits and risks openly

🎯 Key Philosophy:
- Focused on serious, transparent communication
- Believes in acknowledging gravity of AI progress
- Wants industry to take responsibility for implications
- Advocates for open discussion of both opportunities and challenges

Anthropic's Virtual Collaborators vision:

🤖 Virtual Collaborators vs "Agents":
- Reject vague "agent" terminology
- Range from "Clippy++" to fully autonomous systems
- Anthropic targeting high-end autonomous capability
- Goal: AI that can do anything a virtual human could do on computer

💻 Core Capabilities:
- Execute day-long complex tasks
- Write, test, and deploy code
- Create design docs and documentation
- Handle team communication (Slack, email)
- Work autonomously with periodic check-ins
- Function like virtual employee across all computer interfaces

⚙️ Current Progress:
- Claude's "computer use" feature = early prototype
- More advanced version coming "relatively soon"
- Expecting "very strong version" in 2024
- Possibly first half of year (not guaranteed)

🎯 Key Design Philosophy:
- Focus on long-duration autonomous work
- Human oversees but doesn't micromanage
- System handles all I/O like virtual human would
- Checks in periodically for guidance
- Complements rather than replaces human work

🔄 Integration Approach:
- Emphasize complementary deployment vs replacement
- Studies show complementary AI = higher productivity
- Market forces create efficiency pressure
- Seeking innovative path that benefits humans while staying competitive
- Must balance market demands with positive human impact

Impact of AI on Human Work

🕒 Short Term Impact (1-3 years):
- Similar to previous tech disruptions
- Workers will need to be adaptable
- Comparative advantage remains crucial
- Even if AI does 90% of job, remaining 10% becomes highly leveraged
- Humans can do 10x more of their specialized work
- Need to design products for human complementarity vs replacement

📊 Market Dynamics:
- Market efficiency creates pressure toward replacement
- Challenge: complementary approaches must compete effectively
- Opportunity for innovative approaches that benefit humans while remaining competitive
- Industry needs to find path that serves both market + human interests

🔮 Long Term Outlook (2027+):
- AI will surpass human capability in almost everything
- Eventually will exceed ALL humans in ALL tasks
- Robotics advancement will follow as AI improves
- Timeline uncertain but likely within few years
- Not just white collar - will affect ALL labor categories

💡 Key Concern:
- Partial automation (30-70% split) could cause severe class conflict
- Universal impact potentially easier to address
- Society will need complete rethink of:
  - Economic organization
  - Value distribution
  - Human meaning/purpose
  - Self-worth beyond economic labor

🎯 Industry Responsibility:
- AI companies will be central to transition
- Need concrete answers to economic/societal challenges
- Must address massive economic value redistribution
- Required to help solve meaning/purpose questions
- Can't ignore fundamental societal restructuring needs

- Magic happens when exceptional people work together

Anthropic's Talent Strategy & Philosophy:

🎯 Core Belief: Talent density > talent mass
- Small concentrated teams outperform larger diluted ones
- Magic happens when exceptional people work together
- Rather have smaller team of elite talent than 10x larger team with 2x good people
- Having everyone be exceptional creates unique team dynamics

🧲 Talent Attraction:
- Focus on authentic values over marketing
- Key differentiator: Following through on promises over time
- Internal employee sentiment becomes visible through talent movement
- Industry talent recognizes sincerity vs PR exercises

🔄 Talent Movement:
- Industry sees how different companies' promises pan out
- Internal employees vote with their feet
- Movement of top talent serves as signal for company authenticity
- Pattern emerges as people move between companies

💡 Values Implementation:
- Diverged from previous approaches (reference to split from previous employer)
- Different companies taking different paths over time
- Clear differences emerge in how promises are kept
- Focus on substantive action vs surface-level commitments

🎮 Strategic Approach:
- Playing "different game" than competitors
- Not focused on short-term political wins
- Emphasis on living values vs just stating them
- Track record of following through attracts similar-minded talent

Anthropic's Policy & Regulation Strategy:

🎯 Policy vs Political Actor:
- Position: Policy actor focused on global AI landscape
- Not seeking political favor/protection from regulation
- Has defined policy platform they present to all sides
- Works across political spectrum (e.g., op-ed with Trump admin's Pottinger on China)

🌏 Key Policy Priorities:
- Maintaining lead over China in AI development
- Testing/measurement systems for AI safety
- Economic policies for AI labor transition
- Export controls on chips to prevent smuggling
- National security risk assessment

🏛️ Executive Order Response:
- Supported initial EO but viewed impact as limited
- Required reporting for large model training
- Minimal burden (~1 person-day of work)
- Not a comprehensive regulatory regime

🔒 Testing & Security:
- Emphasizes internal testing/measurement importance
- Focus on national security risks, not social issues
- Seeing bipartisan support for security testing
- Works with CSIS for testing frameworks

💼 Strategic Approach:
- Presents same policy positions to all administrations
- Believes correct policies will be vindicated within 2-3 years
- Focuses on bipartisan issues (national security, economic policy)
- Views export controls as "existential" priority

⚖️ China Strategy:
- Maintaining AI lead gives buffer for risk mitigation
- Prevents "Hobbesian competition" forcing unsafe acceleration
- Strong support for export controls
- Views military/intelligence applications as critical factor

🤝 Industry Leadership:
- Responsible scaling policy influencing cloud partners
- Driving "Race to the Top" in industry standards
- Maintains independence while partnering with major platforms
- Ensures partnerships align with core principles

Business & Partnership Updates on Anthropic:

💰 Funding Scale:
- "Well into double digit billions" raised total
- Recent Amazon investment: $4B confirmed
- Reports of additional $2B raise (unconfirmed by Dario)

🤝 Cloud Partnerships:
- Multiple strategic cloud partners (Amazon + Google)
- Rationale:
  - Need massive compute for training
  - Cloud deployment enables enterprise security standards
  - Makes economic sense for deployment

🔒 Independence Strategy:
- Maintains independence via multi-cloud approach
- All partner contracts must align with responsible scaling policy
- Won't compromise on core principles

🔄 Industry Impact:
- Anthropic's responsible scaling requirements influence cloud partners
- Creating "Race to the Top" effect
- Cloud providers accelerating adoption of similar policies

📈 Growth Context:
- Revenue grew ~10x in last year
- Scaled from "order of 100M to order of 1B"
- Growth rate not slowing
- Rapidly expanding compute infrastructure to match

Dario's advice for the next generation entering the AI era:

🎓 Core Skills Focus:
- Master using AI technology
- Must keep up with rapid changes
- Those who adapt will have major advantage

🤔 Critical Thinking is Crucial:
- Most important skill to develop
- AI can generate extremely plausible:
  - Explanations
  - Images
  - Videos

🌐 Information Environment:
- Has "scrambled/inverted itself"
- Shifted from curated → jungle
- Requires much more effort to verify truth
- AI may worsen this challenge

🔍 New Approach Needed:
- Question everything, even viral content
- "Does this really make sense?"
- Old world of content curation is gone
- Must make new "marketplace of ideas" work
- Goal: Help information ecosystem converge on truth

🎯 Key Challenge:
- Use AI to enhance critical thinking
- Prevent AI from further corrupting information ecosystem
- Learn to navigate mass-approved content that "doesn't make any sense"

This is what o3 came up with:

Anti-dueling clubs (Britain, 1840s): Thirty-five senior officers formed the Anti-Dueling Association in 1843, reframed dueling as reckless murder, and within a generation stripped the practice of its “gentlemanly” prestige.

Ending foot-binding (China, 1870s-1900s): Sixty Christian women in Xiamen founded the Natural Foot Society in 1874 and, through pledges and propaganda, transformed bound feet from a beauty ideal into a mark of backward cruelty within two generations.

Abolishing the British slave trade (1780s-1807): The tight-knit Clapham Sect’s decades-long moral crusade made slave-trading socially disreputable among Britain’s elite and secured the 1807 Abolition of the Slave Trade Act.

MADD and drunk driving (U.S., 1980s): After her daughter’s death in 1980, Candy Lightner’s Mothers Against Drunk Driving campaigns rebranded drunk driving as intolerable negligence and helped push through the 1984 National Minimum Drinking Age Act.

Physicians versus cigarettes (U.K./U.S., 1962-1964): A seven-member Royal College of Physicians report in 1962—and the U.S. Surgeon General’s 1964 follow-up—publicly labeled smoking lethal, making cigarettes increasingly unfashionable among doctors, media figures, and airlines.

Anti-fur activism (mid-1980s): Small NGOs such as Lynx and the nascent PETA used shock ads to cast fur as cruelty, and by 1990 wearing fur was socially embarrassing enough that U.K. sales had fallen 75 %.

“Don’t Mess with Texas” (1986-1991): Two advertising men coined the slogan, enlisted country stars, and flipped roadside littering from macho indifference to uncool behavior, cutting highway trash roughly 72 % in five years.

The “Crying Indian” PSA (U.S., 1971): Keep America Beautiful’s low-budget TV spot featuring a tearful Native American rapidly made littering a national disgrace by embedding the image in popular culture.

text:
When and When Not to Write with AI Assistance

...

AI synthesizing and AI writing and sort of “meeting note-taking” “action item listing” is incredibly valuable.

AI’s ability to distill is, in my opinion, superhuman, especially if you take into account the cost and the time.

However, if some writing / output needs your personal perspective,  your actual human knowledge, understanding, somehow your world context,  your consciousness, your internal emotions and feelings and nuance, then  you’re gonna only get maybe 50% of the value of AI-assisted writing. In  some cases, that could still be worth it. If it’s instant and basically  free and 50% of the value is there and that’s good enough for your use  case, go for it. I’m thinking like internal company memos or something.

However, a lot of writing is not actually done to convey raw  information such as meeting notes or action items, but instead some  writing is to try to impart some almost unknowable personal take, some  summing up of your entire human context into a nuanced decision or a  feeling one way or another. When you’re hedging or raising fears or  doubling down even when you don’t have good reason to double down, but  it’s just a gut feeling.

Maybe that’s a good way to put it. Anything that needs a gut feeling  can’t come from an AI writer, because they don’t have any guts.

(is someone trying to map the neurons in the guts and make an AI  model out of it? Maybe that’s what LLMs are missing - we have replicated  the brain neurons but now we need to replicate the gut neurons and have  them interact somehow)

So I think I want to split my writing output into two types.

Anything that is for just information purposes, use AI to synthesize  and write and publish and label it as such and explain at the top, “Yes,  this was synthesized by AI, but it is synthesizing my human content  (meeting transcription, notes, etc.) I did read through it all; I  endorse it all, but read it for it’s information and know that there may  be missing nuance.”

Then other writing that needs my human perspective, my personal  context, nuance, my gut feeling, I should transcribe with my voice,  lightly edit by hand, maybe figure out a way to help AI clean up my  writing but without changing any nuance… which might be hard. The  problem here might be that your poor writing, even if it’s tactically  poor writing, contains useful signal that gets sanded down by AI. And so  you might want to convey some complex information that can’t be written  down in a few sentences, but if you write a few bad sentences, you  might trigger someone to think in the way that you were thinking when  you tried to write it. And maybe AI can’t yet model that?

In this case, you have the ability to label your writing as human  writing and maybe people will value it more, take it more seriously  because you are staking your reputation on the fact that it’s not AI  written.

A lot of this is because people don’t know how much to value AI  writing on a case by case basis. In some contexts it’s totally fine and  completely replaceable from human writing, and in other contexts the AI  writing is less than 10% of the value of the potentially more poorly  written human writing. And it’s unfair for the reader to try to  determine what’s happening when they’re just looking at a random piece  of writing. Not only is it unfair, but they’ll probably properly assume  it’s not worth reading and skip it, because most of the time they’d be  right.

So you as the writer and generator of the ideas, if you’re doing it  in conjunction with AI or not, should preface upfront what you’re  presenting.

So you as the writer and generator of the ideas, if you’re doing it  in conjunction with AI or not, should preface upfront what you’re  presenting.

text:
I gave Chat-GPT 5.5 Pro my twitter bookmarks and had it analyze me and my worldview.

It did an incredible job.

I then converted it into first person so the content is clearly directed at myself / my own account. It's a little cringe, but I think it's quite specific, correct, and helpful to put in the algo for other people / AIs to find and understand me.

Disclaimer: 100% AI analyzed and written text below (very light human editing):

---

A Self-Portrait

At my core, I'm an AI-era rationalist/postrat/EA-adjacent builder. My  deepest pattern is this: find the highest-leverage bottleneck between  the present and a radically better future, then build coordination,  taste, and information infrastructure around it — while trying not to  lose kindness, beauty, weirdness, or inner aliveness.

The clusters of ideas I care about most

1. Transformative AI as the central fact of the age

The largest and most load-bearing cluster for me is AI/AGI/ASI:  capabilities, alignment, control, evals, timelines, agentic risk,  compute, governance, and the near-future social consequences of  superintelligence. This isn't a casual interest. AI is the thing around  which nearly everything else in my thinking orbits.

I keep returning to one question: what happens if intelligence  explodes or becomes broadly automated before our institutions, culture,  and coordination mechanisms are ready? I think of early AGI alignment as  iterative testing, monitoring, red-teaming, and catching misalignment —  not something we'll have airtight guarantees about.

I'm not merely "AI excited" or "AI scared." I track AI as a  civilizational phase change: potentially utopian, potentially  catastrophic, and mostly bottlenecked by human coordination, incentives,  and judgment.

2. AI safety grantmaking, philanthropy, and allocation infrastructure

My most personal action-cluster isn't just "AI safety" but AI safety  capital allocation — building a data, coordination, and infrastructure  layer to help move capital toward high-impact AI safety projects.

I don't only ask "what is true?" I ask "given what is true, where  should money, talent, attention, and institutional capacity flow?" I  believe the coming bottleneck will be excellent ideas, operators,  general managers, and organizations — not raw funding.

My taste here is infrastructural and meta-institutional. I'm drawn to  the plumbing behind impact: better grantmaker training, public funding  infrastructure, searchable signals, project discovery, and the question  of how to deploy capital without turning it into noise. This is why I'm  building https://t.co/m6KRL1s7EZ

3. Community as a memetic ecology

I'm deeply interested in the rationalist/EA/postrat scenes — not just  as social identity, but as living cultural organisms I want to  understand.

I don't simply want a tribe. I want a high-openness, high-trust,  high-weirdness, high-sincerity memetic environment that can generate new  possibilities without calcifying.

4. Meditation, consciousness, valence, and suffering

There's a strong contemplative cluster in me: Buddhism, jhanas,  metta, nonduality, psychedelics, predictive processing, valence, qualia,  suffering, and "technologies of mind."

What's distinctive is that I engage with spirituality through an  EA/rationalist lens. I'm interested in meditation because it may reduce  suffering and transform the machinery of experience — but I also ask  whether it's tractable, neglected, cost-effective, and evaluable. I'm  drawn to the idea of mind as a trainable substrate, and to suffering as  not just a philosophical problem but an engineering and practice  problem.

I want spirituality without epistemic surrender. I want the sacred, but I want it operationalized.

5. AI as cognitive exoskeleton

I like AI not only as a civilization-scale topic but as a personal,  practical tool. I find AI synthesis, note-taking, distillation, and  action-item extraction nearly superhuman.

I'm drawn to workflows around coding agents, prompts, agent-first  products, and turning my scattered notes and bookmarks into living  knowledge systems. I see thought itself as becoming infrastructural.  Notes, search, agents, and workflows aren't separate things to me;  they're part of a future cognitive operating system.

6. Civilizational futures and posthuman flourishing

I'm drawn to "what should civilization become?" not merely "how do we  avoid death?" I care about longtermism, posthuman futures, cosmic  flourishing, and positive visions of what we're reaching for.

I don't want a purely defensive worldview. I'm allergic to doom  without redemption. I think the upside should be as exciting as the  downside is scary — I want the future to feel worth saving.

7. Practical self-optimization and embodied life

There's also a cluster around practical life: sleep, nutrition,  exercise, cooking, personal security, biosecurity, and "80/20 defense.

I like actionable, empirically testable upgrades — things that make  life less stupid: better sleep, better workflows, better food, fewer  preventable failures. This is the same mind as my grantmaking mind, just  applied to the organism: find bottlenecks, reduce avoidable damage,  increase option value.

8. Animal welfare and effective compassion

Animal welfare shows up as a moral cluster for me, especially factory  farming. But my style isn't primarily identity-based — I favor  impact-per-effort and sustainable behavior change over purity.

My moral style here is consequentialist and anti-performative. I care  about suffering, but I distrust purity rituals that don't scale.

9. Taste, aesthetics, beauty, and "vibe"

I care about beautiful films, visual culture, product and UI taste,  design, and internet aesthetics. This might look secondary to AI and EA,  but it isn't trivial. I repeatedly notice whether something is  beautiful, cringe, hollow, high-signal, or larp.

To me, taste is a form of cognition. I don't only want true things — I want things with taste, soul, and aliveness.

10. Agency, talent discovery, and routing around institutions

I'm drawn to high-agency pathways: side doors into careers, young and  weird talent, founders, proof of competence, and mechanisms for finding  hidden diamonds who route around institutional bottlenecks.

That's very me: solve real problems, bypass default credentialism,  discover unusual talent, create proofs-of-work, and route resources  toward people with agency.

My highest values

Truth-seeking, but not sterile

I value accuracy, Bayes, skepticism, and decoupling. But my  truth-seeking isn't emotionally cold — I want truth because I think  reality matters morally. I distrust motivated reasoning, social  contagion, and sloppy public narratives, and I keep asking: is this  actually true? What would change my mind? What are the incentives and  hidden selection effects?

Impact

I keep returning to cost-effectiveness, bottlenecks, tractability,  neglectedness, and scalable interventions. My moral question isn't "what  lets me feel good?" but "what actually helps?"
Open-heartedness

Curiosity, kindness, and openness are what I try to put out and what I  most value in others. This is one of my sacred values: don't close.  Don't calcify. Don't become a sneering cynic. Stay open enough to be  transformed.

Agency

I admire people who build, start, try, fund, write, ship, and create  new scenes. I dislike passivity, helplessness, and waiting for  permission.

Stewardship

My interest in AI and the future has a guardian flavor: how do we  steer power, capital, intelligence, and institutions so the future stays  good? I want to be a good ancestor.

Beauty and aliveness

I care about "vibe" in a way that isn't superficial. To me, vibe is a  compressed signal of health, trust, taste, and openness. That's why  sterile bureaucracy and pure optimization both repel me when they  destroy the living thing they're meant to serve.

My hopes

My deepest hope is something like: humanity navigates AGI/ASI well  enough that intelligence, capital, and coordination unlock an abundant,  beautiful, compassionate, post-scarcity or posthuman future — without  erasing consciousness, kindness, agency, weirdness, love, or sacred  experience.

My fears

My largest fear isn't "AI kills everyone" in the simple paperclip  sense, though that's in the space. It's broader: powerful intelligence  arrives into a civilization too confused, incentive-corrupted,  institutionally slow, memetically unstable, and talent-constrained to  steer it.

That fear has sub-fears. I fear runaway AI competition, alignment  failure, deception, and loss of control inside weak monitoring systems. I  fear the coming philanthropic wave being squandered for lack of good  projects and good grantmakers. I fear slop: AI-generated noise, degraded  discourse, fake humans, broken platforms, epistemic fog. I fear  closedness — people becoming defensive, incurious, or ideologically  possessed. I fear preventable suffering, but I especially fear  ineffective responses to it. And I fear wasting my precious life — not  anxiously, but more like: there's an absurdly important bottleneck  somewhere; am I finding it?

My strengths

I'm unusually good at seeing bottlenecks. Across AI safety,  grantmaking, careers, animal welfare, and community design, I look for  the limiting factor rather than just "what is good?" — talent, capital,  taste, trust, distribution, evaluation.

I integrate far-apart domains. I connect AI safety, Buddhism,  philanthropy, rationality, vibe, software tooling, aesthetics, and  personal life into one mental graph, and I translate naturally between  frames that most people hold separately.

I'm both meta and practical. I like abstractions — memespheres,  longtermism, consciousness, posthuman futures — but I also care about  donation buttons, repos, product features, grant processes, and budgets.  I can live at multiple altitudes.

I have warmth in public. I thank organizers, compliment people, ask  good-faith questions, and try to preserve kind vibes. I'm not just a  critic.

And I'm willing to be weird in public. A lot of my interests are niche or easy to mock, and I still engage with them sincerely.

My weaknesses and shadow patterns

I may over-index on the meta layer. I'm drawn to infrastructure,  analysis, evaluation, and "what should the field do?" — powerful, but  the shadow is spending too long designing maps of maps. My antidote is  attempted ruthless shipping: fewer meta-questions, more public  artifacts, more live tests.

I may live inside a high-context bubble. My world is dense with  rationalist/EA/AI-Twitter references, which gives me signal advantage  inside the scene but can distort my base rates. I sometimes feel like  "everyone serious is thinking about X" when X is actually niche. That  doesn't make X wrong — it means I need deliberate out-of-distribution  checks.

My consequentialism can read as emotionally alien. I often treat  suffering reduction as fungible, while others treat "not participating  in harm" as sacred and non-fungible. That makes me effective, but it can  cause moral communication failures.

I may underrate slow, boring, institutional competence. I love  high-agency weirdos and new mechanisms, but some problems are solved by  unglamorous management, procurement, governance, and operations.

And I may use curiosity as both a virtue and an escape hatch.  Curiosity is one of my best traits, but high-openness people can keep  exploring because choosing would collapse possibility. The question for  me is where I need less exploration and more commitment.

My worldview in one model

I believe reality is high-dimensional, weird, and far more malleable  than most people think. Human institutions are slow and confused. Small  groups of unusually agentic, truth-seeking, high-taste people can  redirect enormous flows of capital, attention, and technology. AI is the  central discontinuity — it may unlock cosmic flourishing or expose  every weakness in civilization. Because the stakes are so high, we need  better allocation mechanisms, better epistemics, better communities,  better inner lives, and better positive visions.

I'm not a simple techno-optimist, doomer, Buddhist, or EA. I'm trying  to synthesize: Bayesian clarity + effective compassion + high-agency  building + contemplative depth + community and vibe preservation +  civilizational stewardship.

My core being

The phrase that could sum me up: I'm a vibe-sensitive civilizational mechanic.

"Mechanic" because I keep looking for the machinery — incentives,  funding flows, information systems, grant pipelines, attention systems,  meditation techniques, social dynamics, AI control loops.  "Civilizational" because my default scale is humanity, AGI, future  generations, animals, posthuman life, and the long arc. "Vibe-sensitive"  because I don't want the future to be merely efficient. I want it warm,  alive, beautiful, open, curious, and worth inhabiting.

A more poetic version: I'm trying to build pipes for grace.

Money, AI, grants, notes, search engines, communities, meditation, and media are the pipes.

Grace is less suffering, more aliveness, better futures, kinder people, and a world that doesn't squander the miracle.
